questions.scenario:
A deploy pipeline fails before reloading Nginx:

```text
$ nginx -t
nginx: [emerg] unknown directive "proxy_passs" in /etc/nginx/sites-available/api.conf:14
nginx: configuration file /etc/nginx/nginx.conf test failed
```

What is the precise failing location?

options.text: `/etc/nginx/sites-available/api.conf:14`
`/var/log/nginx/access.log:14`
`/etc/hosts:443`
The validator output does not include a file or line number

questions.explanation: The config test reports the bad directive and exact source location. The file and line are `/etc/nginx/sites-available/api.conf:14`.

questions.scenario:
Line 14 in the `/api/v2/` block is:

```nginx
proxy_passs http://127.0.0.1:3001;
```

Which replacement unblocks `nginx -t` while preserving the upstream target?

options.text: `proxy_passs http://127.0.0.1:3001;`
`proxy_set_header http://127.0.0.1:3001;`

questions.explanation: The directive is misspelled with an extra `s`. The upstream target should stay `http://127.0.0.1:3001;`, so only the directive name changes to `proxy_pass`.

questions.scenario:
A different deploy fails to start Nginx with:

```text
nginx: [emerg] bind() to 0.0.0.0:80 failed (98: Address already in use)
```

Which next check best matches the article's troubleshooting guidance?

options.text: Use `ss -tlnp` filtered for port 80 to find the process already listening
Change `try_files` because SPA fallbacks reserve port 80
Delete the TLS certificate because bind errors mean certificate permission denied
Lower the DNS TTL because stale cache can occupy a local port

questions.explanation: The article's port-in-use failure mode shows this exact bind error and recommends checking listeners with `ss -tlnp`. The problem is another process on the port, not DNS, SPA routing, or certificate permissions.

questions.scenario:
Nginx is running, but users get:

```text
HTTP/1.1 502 Bad Gateway
```

The error log says:

```text
connect() failed (111: Connection refused) while connecting to upstream, upstream: "http://127.0.0.1:3000/users"
```

What is the most likely cause?

options.text: The upstream app is not running on the expected address and port, or it is listening somewhere else
The client sent a malformed JSON body, so Nginx could not parse it
The browser blocked CORS before reaching Nginx
The static `root` path lacks an `index.html` file

questions.explanation: The article defines 502 as Nginx reaching for an upstream that is not there or returns invalid data. A connection-refused upstream log points at the app process or port binding.

questions.scenario: A report says Nginx returns 504 for `/reports/export`. The backend is reachable, but the request takes about 90 seconds and the proxy timeout is 60 seconds. What is the best interpretation?

options.text: The upstream did not respond within Nginx's timeout window, so diagnose backend latency before only raising the timeout
The upstream sent malformed HTTP immediately, which is always a 502
The Nginx config has an unknown directive, so it never started
The certificate file permissions are wrong because 504 is a TLS-only error

questions.explanation: The article distinguishes 504 from 502: the upstream is reachable but too slow to respond within the allowed time. Increasing timeout may be needed, but the backend latency is the real investigation.

questions.scenario: An on-call follows the article's quick diagnostic checklist for an Nginx outage. Which checks belong in that first pass?

options.text: Check whether Nginx is running, for example with `systemctl status nginx`
Validate the config with `nginx -t`
Inspect recent Nginx error logs
Test whether the backend is reachable with a local `curl`
Inspect listening ports with `ss -tlnp`
Rotate every DNS nameserver before checking local evidence

questions.explanation: The quick checklist is process status, config validity, logs, backend reachability, and listening ports. DNS can be relevant in some incidents, but rotating nameservers before checking local Nginx evidence is not the checklist.

questions.scenario:
After a certificate renewal, `nginx -t` fails with:

```text
nginx: [emerg] SSL_CTX_use_certificate_chain("/etc/letsencrypt/live/example.com/fullchain.pem") failed
  (SSL: error:0200100D:system library:fopen:Permission denied)
```

Which cause best matches the article?

options.text: The Nginx master process cannot read the certificate chain path because ownership or permissions were changed incorrectly
The upstream app is too slow, so Nginx should raise `proxy_read_timeout`
The port is already in use, so Nginx cannot bind to `0.0.0.0:80`
The SPA fallback should point to `/index.html` because certificate files are static assets

questions.explanation: The article's certificate permission failure shows this error pattern. The fix is to verify permissions and ownership so the Nginx master process can read the certificate files before workers drop privileges.
